coredata.dc:description: Effective thermal management is critical for ensuring the safety, performance, and lifespan of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), particularly in high-demand applications such as electric vehicles. This study presents a comparative experimental evaluation of five battery thermal management strategies—natural and forced air convection, cold plate cooling, and single-phase (3 M™ NOVEC™ 7100) and two-phase (3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000) immersion cooling, each tested without and with porous structures to enhance coolant distribution and boiling behaviour. A distinguishing feature of this work is the use of a realistic 24-cell (12S2P) battery pack, significantly larger than typical laboratory-scale tests, enabling a more practical assessment of intra-pack thermal gradients. Temperatures were recorded from seven spatially distributed locations to capture nonuniformities within the pack during operation. A key innovation is the integration of porous compression pads within immersion-cooled configurations. These structures enhance fluid transport, improve capillary liquid retention, and promote vapor venting, resulting in marked improvements in thermal uniformity. Among the methods tested, natural convection resulted in excessive peak temperatures (>60 °C) and poor distribution, while forced convection provided only marginal gains. Cold plates showed localized cooling but failed to address internal localised thermal gradients effectively. Single-phase immersion cooling with porous media improved uniformity but showed elevated surface temperatures due to limited conductive pathways through the porous layer. In contrast, two-phase immersion cooling, enhanced by latent heat effects and porous structures, achieved the lowest maximum temperatures (<36 °C) and the most uniform thermal profile. These findings establish two-phase immersion cooling with porous enhancement as a scalable, effective, and safety-oriented solution for next-generation battery systems, offering improved performance, lifespan, and integration potential for real-world applications.

Summary of Recent Experimental and Numerical Studies on Immersion Cooling Techniques for Battery Thermal Management.

row.entry: 18,650 cylindrical cell and 4S2P battery pack tested at 2C & 3C discharge rates using an experimental setup. The study focused on steady-state convection-based enhanced oil immersion cooling (EOIC) to evaluate thermal management effectiveness.

Prismatic battery packs simulated at 3C & 5C discharge rates using a 3D transient numerical model. The study compared the thermal performance of different immersion liquids through detailed simulations.
Numerical CFD study on a 1P36S battery configuration with a novel spray immersion cooling structure. The work explores effective heat removal using forced spray cooling.

Experimental study on 26,650 LiFePO4 cylindrical batteries under both charge and discharge. The research utilized direct-contact immersion cooling through a membrane contactor, enhancing phase change heat transfer.
Experimental study of prismatic Li–ion cells immersed in dielectric nanofluid; compared effects on thermal response for both single–phase and two–phase conditions.

A numerical study used multi–objective genetic algorithm to optimize biomimetic internal flow channels, improving temperature uniformity and reducing peak temperatures in an immersed Li–ion battery pack.

Designed a fish-shaped bionic cooling channel to improve flow distribution and thermal performance of a Li-ion battery pack under immersion cooling. Demonstrated enhanced temperature uniformity and reduced thermal resistance compared to conventional straight-channel designs.

Comparison of natural convection (single-phase) and boiling (two-phase) modes using a 26,650 LiFePO4 four-cell module under up to 4C charge/discharge. The study demonstrated significant cooling performance differences.
Numerical investigation of 4S2P lithium-ion battery pack under 3C discharge. The paper analysed dielectric fluid immersion cooling using computational methods.
Methyl silicone oil, white oil, transformer oil
A comparative study of various oil-based coolants for 2S2P battery modules under 2C discharge. Cooling effectiveness and thermal uniformity were examined; method details not specified.
Experimental testing on 4S2P LIB pack under 3C discharge. Performance was evaluated under varying temperature conditions for different oil types.
Study used a 50Ah ternary LIB (LISHEN, LP2714898). Likely focused on leveraging phase change for improved thermal management.
Numerical 3D model simulation of a 60-cell battery pack under 2C discharge, focusing on immersion cooling performance with this specific coolant.
Comprehensive overview of BTMS advances, including immersion cooling; highlights emerging designs, coolants, modelling techniques, and performance benchmarks.
Experimental comparison of mineral and synthetic oils on a 5S4P battery pack at 1C–3C discharge rates. The paper evaluated thermal management performance and coolant suitability.
Experimental fast-charging study (3C) using six common LIBs. Two-phase cooling was evaluated for its ability to manage rapid heat generation.
Sony VTC6 cylindrical battery (3P7S, 21 cells) tested at 2C and 3C charge rates. Experimental evaluation of two-phase immersion cooling’s efficiency.
Six-cell cylindrical Li-ion module tested under varying C-rates. Likely involving thermal profiling under immersion.
Experimental work on 18,650 cylindrical cells under various discharge rates, analysing temperature distribution and variations in immersion cooling setups.
SONY VTC6 18,650 LIB studied under 4C discharge. The study likely explored two-phase cooling through latent heat extraction during boiling.
54 V LiFePO4 pack (18 pouch cells in series) tested under 10C discharge rate. The study demonstrates two-phase cooling under high load.
Experimental work on a single LiFePO4 cell with 3C charging, employing direct immersion to analyze phase change heat removal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective thermal management is critical for ensuring the safety, performance, and lifespan of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), particularly in high-demand applications such as electric vehicles. This study presents a comparative experimental evaluation of five battery thermal management strategies—natural and forced air convection, cold plate cooling, and single-phase (3 M™ NOVEC™ 7100) and two-phase (3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000) immersion cooling, each tested without and with porous structures to enhance coolant distribution and boiling behaviour. A distinguishing feature of this work is the use of a realistic 24-cell (12S2P) battery pack, significantly larger than typical laboratory-scale tests, enabling a more practical assessment of intra-pack thermal gradients. Temperatures were recorded from seven spatially distributed locations to capture nonuniformities within the pack during operation. A key innovation is the integration of porous compression pads within immersion-cooled configurations. These structures enhance fluid transport, improve capillary liquid retention, and promote vapor venting, resulting in marked improvements in thermal uniformity. Among the methods tested, natural convection resulted in excessive peak temperatures (>60 °C) and poor distribution, while forced convection provided only marginal gains. Cold plates showed localized cooling but failed to address internal localised thermal gradients effectively. Single-phase immersion cooling with porous media improved uniformity but showed elevated surface temperatures due to limited conductive pathways through the porous layer. In contrast, two-phase immersion cooling, enhanced by latent heat effects and porous structures, achieved the lowest maximum temperatures (<36 °C) and the most uniform thermal profile. These findings establish two-phase immersion cooling with porous enhancement as a scalable, effective, and safety-oriented solution for next-generation battery systems, offering improved performance, lifespan, and integration potential for real-world applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing demand for high-performance lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) for use in electric vehicles (EVs), renewable energy storage, and portable electronics highlights the importance of effective thermal management systems (BTMSs). These systems are essential to maintain optimal battery performance, ensure safety, and extend the lifespan of LIBs, which are highly sensitive to temperature variations. Effective thermal management prevents thermal runaway (TR) and enhances the uniformity of the temperature distribution, which is critical for the durability and efficiency of battery packs [1,2].

Among all cooling methods, air cooling systems are the earliest and most widely adopted BTMSs, owing to their simplicity, low cost, and lightweight design [3]. These systems rely on natural or forced convection to dissipate heat generated during battery operation [4–7]. While suitable for low to moderate heat loads of about 5 to 20 W/cm2, air cooling suffers from inherent limitations such as the low thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity of air, which result in poor heat dissipation and significant temperature gradients within the battery pack [8,9]. Such temperature heterogeneities accelerate uneven cell degradation and reduce the overall pack performance and lifespan [10,11]. Despite advancements in air cooling, such as optimizing airflow patterns and cell arrangements [12–15], these systems remain insufficient for the high thermal demands (exceeding 20 W/cm2) of modern electric vehicles and fast-charging applications [16,17].

Both indirect and direct liquid cooling have emerged as a more efficient alternative to air cooling [18–20]. Indirect liquid cooling systems use fluid-filled channels [21], jackets [22], or plates [23] in contact with the battery pack to absorb and remove heat. Common coolants include water-glycol mixtures, which offer significantly higher heat transfer coefficients than air [24,25]. However, these systems face challenges related to design complexity, potential fluid leakage, and increased weight [26,27]. Indirect cooling often fails to achieve uniform temperature regulation across the pack, especially under high power density or fast-charging conditions [28,29].

Direct liquid cooling systems, such as immersion cooling, place the battery cells in direct contact with a dielectric fluid [30–34]. Single-phase immersion cooling has demonstrated more heat transfer enhancements, temperature reduction, and uniform heat dissipation than air cooling [35–38]. However, the reliance on convective heat transfer in single-phase systems may not be sufficient for extreme heat loads (50 to 100 W/cm2 or higher) encountered in emerging high-energy–density LIB applications [39]​. These limitations have driven the search efforts for alternative strategies, which focus on two-phase heat transfer systems being a promising solution​.

Two-phase immersion cooling represents a significant step forward in BTMS design, leveraging the latent heat of vaporization and phase change mechanisms to enhance heat dissipation [40–44]. Related studies on two-phase systems have revealed their potential to achieve orders-of-magnitude improvements in heat transfer rates compared to single-phase systems [45–47]. For instance, refrigerant-based boiling and immersion boiling techniques have shown promising results in maintaining uniform temperatures and reducing localised thermal gradients in LIB packs [48]​. Additionally, experimental studies have highlighted the ability of these systems to suppress thermal runaway and improve overall safety by acting as emergency heat sinks [49].

To provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of immersion cooling in battery thermal management, Table 1 presents a curated list of the most recent experimental and numerical investigations in this field. This collection spans the latest developments and captures a wide range of cooling configurations, dielectric fluids (including oils, Novec™, and refrigerants), and battery cell formats (cylindrical, pouch, prismatic). It offers a detailed snapshot of recent trends in both single-phase and two-phase immersion cooling strategies, highlighting the diversity in experimental setups, coolant types, and battery configurations used in prior research.

Furthermore, recent advances in immersion liquid cooling across electronics and data centre applications offer valuable insights relevant to battery thermal management. Studies have demonstrated that fluorinated dielectric liquids enable effective single-phase cooling by providing high thermal stability, low viscosity, and electrical insulation, making them ideal for compact high-power systems [67,68]. Moreover, experimental investigations on high-power server cooling confirm the capability of immersion systems to maintain uniform temperature profiles and improve heat dissipation efficiency [30,69]. A broader review of immersion cooling materials, system designs, and emerging applications emphasizes its scalability, safety, and environmental benefits, supporting its growing adoption in high-heat-flux scenarios [70,71]. These developments reinforce the motivation to adapt and evaluate such technologies for advanced battery thermal management.

Despite growing interest in immersion cooling—particularly due to the high heat transfer potential of two-phase systems—the existing literature exhibits several critical limitations. Many studies investigate overly simplified battery configurations with only a few cells or focus narrowly on specific aspects of cooling performance, often under idealized conditions. Moreover, comparative analyses of different battery thermal management system (BTMS) methods under consistent and realistic experimental conditions are scarce, hindering the ability to draw definitive conclusions for practical applications.

To address these gaps, the present study conducts a comprehensive, comparative experimental analysis of two-phase immersion cooling using 3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000, benchmarked against natural air cooling, forced air cooling, cooling plates (indirect liquid cooling), and single-phase immersion cooling. Importantly, experiments are performed on a 24-cell lithium-ion battery pack, offering a much more realistic representation of battery systems used in electric vehicles and energy storage applications. Additionally, the study provides detailed insights into temperature distribution across the battery pack, utilizing seven thermocouples strategically placed at different locations to capture spatial temperature variations.

A key novel contribution of this work is the integration of porous structures within the immersion cooling setups, implemented in both single-phase and two-phase systems. These structures enhance fluid motion and vapor transport, significantly improving temperature uniformity and reducing thermal gradients across the battery pack. Additionally, this study introduces gravimetric and volumetric thermal performance metrics critical for weight and volume-constrained applications such as electric vehicles. These benchmarks, rarely addressed in existing immersion cooling literature, provide a practical means to evaluate and compare the thermal efficiency of compact battery cooling systems. These improvements are crucial for battery safety, performance consistency, and lifespan extension. By demonstrating the superior thermal performance of the two-phase system through latent heat utilization and enhanced convective heat transfer in a dielectric environment, this work lays the foundation for the development of next-generation, high-efficiency, and scalable battery thermal management solutions.

In this study, 18,650 lithium-ion batteries were used due to their widespread application in electric vehicles. The battery pack consists of 24 cells arranged (Table 2 - in a configuration of 12 series and 2 parallel (12S2P)), as shown in Fig. 1
a. The specifications of the individual cells and the battery pack are detailed in Fig. 1 b. The battery pack has a Battery Management System (BMS) to ensure safe and efficient operation. The BMS was designed to prevent overcharging and over-discharging and to regulate voltage by disconnecting the battery if the temperature exceeds safe thresholds (the safe temperature range for charging is usually between 0 °C to 45° and −20 °C to 60 °C for discharging). Additionally, the BMS protects against common electronic hazards, including voltage spikes (overshoot), short circuits, and open circuits.

The air-cooling setup includes the battery pack with thermocouples installed on the cells (configured as shown in Fig. 2 ), a fan, an ITECH bi-directional DC power supply, and a separate DC power supply allocated for the 24 V air fan (96 Cubic Feet per Minute, 2.7 Cubic Meters per Minute). Two types of air-cooling experiments were conducted: natural convection, where the fan was not activated, and forced convection, where the fan was operational. Before starting with these tests, the battery pack was fully charged and allowed to stabilize at room temperature to reach a steady state. Once this condition was achieved, the discharge process commenced at a current of 6.8A, with temperature readings recorded continuously using an 8-channel handheld thermocouple Thermometer /Data Logger (OM-HL-EH-TC/Omega Engineering).

In the cold plate configuration, a plate (aluminium) with a high thermal conductivity with channels through which a coolant circulates was attached to the battery pack (Fig. 3
a). The cold plate cooling method offers a more uniform temperature distribution and the capacity to manage higher heat loads, making it ideal for high-heat load applications. The contact between the cold plate and battery cells ensures efficient heat transfer, helping in maintaining optimal operating temperatures while enhancing the cell safety and lifespan. Three configurations of the cold plate with the battery pack were tested: (1) the cold plate placed beneath the battery pack, (2) the cold plate positioned on top of the battery pack, and (3) the battery pack sandwiched between two cold plates—one at the top and one at the bottom. Fig. 3 b provides a schematic representation of these configurations. The tests were conducted sequentially, starting with the cold plate on top of the battery pack, followed by placement underneath, and finally, on both sides of the battery pack. The schematic of the experimental setup for cold plate tests is shown in Fig. 4 . The experimental setup consists of the battery pack, thermocouples in the same locations, the ITECH bi-directional DC power supply, cold plates, and a chiller.

The selected coolants for this study are 3 M™ Novec™ 7100 for single-phase cooling and 3 M™ Novec™ 7000 for two-phase cooling tests. Different fluids are used because the boiling points are critical to their cooling performance: 3 M™ Novec™ 7000 (boiling point 34 °C) quickly transitions to two-phase cooling as battery temperatures reach 34 °C, while 3 M™ Novec™ 7100 (boiling point 61 °C) remains in the single-phase, ensuring effective cooling without premature phase change. The hydrofluoroether compounds are eco-friendly, with low global warming potential and zero ozone depletion potential. Additionally, they are transparent, non-flammable, thermally stable, and suitable for various thermal management applications. Their properties are summarized in Table 3 .

The porous compression pad (polyurethane foam with k = 0.01 – 0.3 W/m.K, average pore size of 50 – 100 μm and porosity of 60 – 80 %) was introduced between the battery cells and battery cell walls. The porous media were inserted during the packing process. Fig. 5 shows the contact angle on the porous structure for 5 s under the thermodynamic equilibrium condition (chamber filled with the NOVEC™7000 vapor). As can be seen, the dielectric coolant completely absorbs the porous structure, and the thickness of the porous structure (compression pads) was 3 mm.

Although long-term exposure to fluorinated fluids has been reported to affect polyurethane materials in some studies [72], no degradation, leakage, or structural changes were observed during the multiple full discharge cycles in our test period. The foam maintained its integrity and functionality throughout the experimental timeline.

In two-phase immersion cooling, the coolant absorbs sensible heat and utilizes the phase change from liquid to vapor to achieve a superior thermal performance. Fig. 6 shows the schematic of the two-phase immersion cooling method experimental setup. It consists of a pump, reservoir, preheater, test section, heat exchanger, pressure and temperature sensors, a bi-directional DC power supply, and a flow meter. 3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000 introduces this phase change mechanism with its saturation temperature of 34 °C at a mass flow rate of 300 ml/min and atmospheric pressure. As the battery cells heat up during operation, the coolant reaches its saturation temperature, and the subsequent phase change absorbs a significant amount of heat, effectively limiting the maximum temperature rise. The phase change experiments were tested at an inlet temperature of 32 °C which is close to the saturation temperature. For comparison purposes, the single-phase immersion tests were also performed using 3 M™ NOVEC™7100 at the inlet temperature of 32 °C and inlet flow rate of 300 ml/min.

Similar to the single-phase immersion cooling test, the same porous structures were also used inside the battery pack in two-phase immersion cooling tests. The phase change tests were performed in the presence of compression pads.

No prescriptive thermal pass/fail thresholds (e.g., specific Tmax or ΔT limits) were imposed a priori. Each cooling method was evaluated under identical electrical and thermal boundary conditions at 1C, and the resulting Tmax and ΔT were reported as measured outcomes for comparative analysis. To ensure the reliability and repeatability of the results, all experiments described in this study were conducted three times under identical operating conditions. The thermal responses, temperature distributions, and performance measurements reported throughout the Results and Discussion section represent the average values obtained across these repeated trials. The observed variation among repetitions remained within the experimental uncertainty margins detailed in the following section.

An uncertainty analysis based on the error propagation methodology proposed by Coleman and Steele [73] was performed. Accordingly, the general formulation of the uncertainty in the derived parameter is expressed as:

Where xout is vapor quality at the outlet and is calculated based on the enthalpy of the saturated liquid (if,sat), and the enthalpy of vaporization (ifg), and the pressure and temperature at the test section inlet and outlet.

“This study assessed reproducibility by repeating each cooling configuration on three independent days (n = 3). For each configuration, the following quantities are reported: (i) peak cell temperature, T_max; (ii) pack non-uniformity, ΔT = T_max-T_minat steady state; and (iii) the mean ± standard deviation (SD) and coefficient of variation, CV = 100 × SD/mean. SD uses Bessel’s correction. Where 95 % confidence intervals (CI) are shown, they are computed from the t-distribution with df = n-1 = 2. Instrument calibration uncertainty for temperature is ± 1°C; the table reports repeatability (SD across runs) separately from instrument uncertainty for transparency. Table 5 summarises the room-temperature test results.

Tmax reproducibility is high across methods (CV 2.4–4.1 %). The larger CVs for ΔT, especially in single-phase immersion, reflect the small absolute ΔT (≈2 K) relative to run-to-run variability rather than poor stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gravimetric and volumetric thermal performance metrics
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical efficiency of the proposed cooling system, we introduce two performance metrics:

Gravimetric Thermal Performance

where Qremoved is the effective heat removed by the cooling system, m is the total system mass, and V is the total system volume including coolant, porous insert, tubing, and reservoir. These metrics enable direct comparison between systems with different size and mass constraints.

To further quantify the thermal performance of the system, dimensionless parameters such as the Nusselt number (Nu) and the Biot number (Bi) were evaluated. These parameters enable generalized comparisons with other systems, regardless of scale or geometry.

The Nusselt number is defined as,
(7)
Nu =
h
L c
k f
Where h is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m2·K), Lc is the characteristic length (m), and kf is the thermal conductivity of the working fluid (W/m·K). A high Nusselt number indicates efficient convective heat transfer relative to conduction in the fluid. Here, the convective heat transfer coefficient is calculated using the h =
Q
in
N . A . Δ T
equation, where Qin is the total heat generated by the battery pack (W), N is the number of battery cells, A is the surface area of a single cell (m2), and ΔT is the average temperature difference between the cell surface and the working fluid (K). The coolant temperature is estimated by a linear energy balance, assuming that the heat absorbed by the fluid causes a linear temperature rise from the inlet (Tin) to the outlet (Tout). The Biot number is given by Eq. (8) as follows,
(8)
Bi =
h
L c
k s
In this equation, h is again the convective heat transfer coefficient and ks​ is the thermal conductivity of the solid material (W/m·K). A Biot number Bi < 0.1 implies negligible internal thermal resistance in the solid, justifying the lumped capacitance assumption and confirming uniform temperature distribution within the battery.

This section presents the results obtained from the different cooling methods of lithium-ion battery packs, focusing on their thermal performance. The performance evaluation encompasses charging and discharging cycles and examination of the heat transfer performance, including maximum and average temperatures and temperature gradients for each method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Natural and forced convection air cooling
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the surface temperature variations of different battery cells during discharge at the C-rate of 1C for natural convection at an ambient temperature of 23 °C. Natural air convection cooling is the simplest method for the thermal management of batteries. It depends on the temperature gradient between the battery and ambient air to dissipate heat. However, this method has two significant disadvantages. Firstly, the thermal energy generated within the battery cells leads to elevated temperatures, reaching approximately 60 °C. High temperatures can negatively affect battery performance, reduce efficiency, and pose safety risks, including thermal runaway. Secondly, the heat dissipation is uneven, with the inner battery cells experiencing higher temperatures due to their lower heat transfer coefficients than the outer cells. For instance, the inner cells (top centre, bottom centre, and pack centre) exhibit a temperature difference of more than 4 °C between their top and bottom points, primarily because the heat radiated from surrounding cells raises its temperature. This non-uniform temperature distribution accelerates cell aging and reduces the overall lifespan of the battery pack. Additionally, the effectiveness of natural air cooling is heavily dependent on ambient conditions, which might vary and often be insufficient to maintain optimal battery health.

Cold plates are widely adopted for battery systems. For simplicity, temperature readings from T2 (Top cnt.) and T5 (Bottom cnt.) were omitted from the results. Throughout the experiments, the cold plate temperature was consistently maintained at the temperature of 10 °C. A 10 °C inlet temperature for the cold plate ensures effective heat removal, maintaining battery temperatures within the optimal range during high heat generation scenarios such as rapid charging or discharging. Additionally, this temperature replicates extreme cooling requirements, allowing for the evaluation of the cold plate's thermal management efficiency under demanding conditions.

The cold plate was tested in three configurations: positioned on top of the battery pack, placed underneath, and on both sides of the battery pack. The experimental results, shown in Fig. 8
a–c, reveal that the cold plate significantly reduces the temperature, maintaining the battery pack in the range of approximately 32–34 °C. This represents a notable improvement over air-based cooling methods. The setup with cold plates on both the top and bottom surfaces achieves the most uniform temperature distribution among the tested configurations. However, this arrangement presents practical challenges, such as integrating cold plates throughout the system without significantly increasing its weight and complexity.

Furthermore, Fig. 8 c highlights the thermal limitation of cold plate cooling. While the top and bottom parts of the battery pack are effectively cooled to low and uniform temperatures, the central region (Pack cnt.) remains relatively hot due to its distance from the cold plates. This uneven cooling underscores the limitations of the cold plate method in achieving consistent thermal management for all the cells. These findings suggest that exploring alternative cooling methods, such as immersion cooling, could offer more uniform temperature control across the entire battery pack.

In single-phase immersion cooling tests, the 3 M™ Novec™ 7100 coolant was circulated through the battery pack to absorb and dissipate heat generated by the cells. Fig. 9
a shows the temperature profile of the battery pack during a 1C discharge cycle, with the coolant entering at room temperature. As 3 M™ Novec™ 7100 absorbs heat from the cell surfaces, its temperature rises, effectively reducing the cell temperatures. Compared to natural and forced convection air cooling methods, single-phase immersion cooling achieves a significantly lower maximum temperature, approximately 28 °C. This substantial reduction is due to the enhanced heat transfer coefficient of the liquid coolant. Unlike cold plate and air-cooling methods, single-phase immersion cooling provides an outstandingly uniform temperature distribution, a critical advantage over previous methods. The temperature difference across the pack—between cells at the top (T1, T2, T3), bottom (T4, T5, T6), and centre (Pack cnt.) is nearly 2 °C, highlighting its consistent cooling performance. Such uniformity is critical for extending battery cell life, as it minimizes thermal gradients that can accelerate uneven aging and potential failure.

Fig. 9 b illustrates the effect of single-phase immersion cooling in a porous structure (compression pad). The porous structure further reduces temperature differences between battery cells, enhancing cooling uniformity. This improvement is attributed to the wicking effect, which aids in distributing the coolant more evenly across the cells, ensuring efficient and consistent heat removal (Fig. 11).

Fig. 9 c and Fig. 9 d depict the temperature profiles for single-phase immersion cooling with an inlet temperature of 32 °C. While the porous structure enhances temperature uniformity, it comes with the drawback of elevated surface temperatures due to the low thermal conductivity of the compression pad used. This limitation can be mitigated using a porous structure with higher thermal conductivity. The cooling efficiency is significantly influenced by the battery pack design and the flow dynamics of the coolant. For instance, in the central region of the pack (Pack cnt.), the narrow 2 mm gaps between cells restrict fluid flow, reducing the local heat transfer coefficient and leading to a slight temperature rise. Conversely, cells in the first rows ('Top in' and 'Bottom in'), which are directly exposed to the incoming coolant flow, exhibit lower temperatures. These observations emphasize the need for optimizing flow pathways to ensure effective and uniform cooling throughout the battery pack. The saturation temperature of 3 M™ Novec™ 7100 is 61 °C at atmospheric pressure, and since the maximum temperature in this test remains below this threshold, the cooling process stays within the single-phase regime. To investigate the potential advantages of two-phase cooling, which leverages the latent heat of vaporization for enhanced thermal management, 3 M™ Novec™ 7000 was selected as the coolant for subsequent experiments due to its lower saturation temperature of 34 °C.

In two-phase immersion cooling, the coolant absorbs heat and utilizes its latent heat during the phase change from liquid to vapor, enabling superior heat removal. 3 M™ Novec™ 7000 was selected to investigate the phase change heat transfer performance due to its low saturation temperature. As the battery cells heat up during operation, the coolant reaches its saturation temperature and absorbs significant heat, effectively limiting the maximum temperature rise. Fig. 10
a shows the temperature profiles of the cells at various times during a 1C discharge cycle. A preheater was used to maintain a fixed inlet temperature of 31 °C to achieve the saturation temperature of 3 M™ Novec™ 7000 which is 34 °C.

As shown in Fig. 10 a, whenever the surface temperature approaches 36 °C, it stabilizes. This effective thermal management highlights the coolant's ability to efficiently regulate the battery pack's temperature. However, occasional temperature spikes above 36 °C could be observed, likely due to the presence of the secondary vapor phase. The preheater plays a critical role in ensuring that the coolant enters the battery pack at a temperature close to its saturation temperature, optimizing the efficiency of the phase change process. The latent heat of vaporization of 3 M™ Novec™ 7000 enables it to absorb substantial amounts of heat during the liquid-to-vapor transition, stabilizing the temperature of the battery cells and maintaining optimal operating conditions.

Fig. 10 b illustrates the temperature profiles in the presence of a porous structure. The results demonstrate that the porous structure achieves the lowest temperature fluctuations among all tested thermal management approaches, highlighting its superiority in temperature regulation. Compared to single-phase immersion cooling with a porous structure, which achieves uniform temperatures at higher overall cell temperatures, the phase change process provides the most uniform temperature distribution during discharge. The impact of the porous structure on temperature fluctuations in the two-phase regime is further depicted in Fig. 10 c. The presence of the porous structure enhances the coolant distribution and mitigates localized temperature variations, optimizing the thermal management performance during phase change cooling. The observed improvement in temperature uniformity and reduction in peak temperature can be attributed to the enhanced capillary wicking and increased nucleation site density provided by the porous foam, which promotes more efficient phase change and boiling heat transfer. This behaviour is consistent with classic boiling heat transfer mechanisms, where surface roughness and wettability influence the nucleation threshold and bubble dynamics [74].

To further support the thermal performance analysis, we estimated the interstitial velocity of the coolant within the porous domain using volumetric and material parameters from the experimental setup. The battery pack enclosure has internal dimensions of 18 cm × 12.5 cm × 10 cm, yielding a total volume of Vbox = 0.00225 m3. The volume occupied by the 24 cylindrical 18,650 cells (D = 3.2 cm, H = 7 cm) is approximately Vbatt = 0.00135 m3. The remaining space, filled with porous foam—constitutes the porous volume (8.99 × 10−4m3).

The coolant flow rate was maintained at Q = 300 mL/min. Assuming porosity ε in the range of 0.6–0.8, the interstitial velocity Ui (representing fluid speed within the pores) is calculated as:
(9)
U i
=
U s
ε .
V
porous
Substituting values gives:
•
For ε = 0.8: Ui = 6.95 × 10−3 m/s
•
For ε = 0.6: Ui = 9.27 × 10−3 m/s
These values indicate a low-Reynolds-number regime, where viscous and capillary effects dominate.

Beyond flow velocity, pore size strongly influences the hydrodynamic and thermal behaviour of the porous medium, particularly in two-phase cooling environments. In our study, the polyurethane foam had an average pore size of 50–100 µm, which plays a dual role:

Flow resistance: Smaller pores increase viscous drag and reduce permeability, following empirical models such as the Kozeny-Carman relation:
(10)
K ∝
ε .
d
p
2
C
where dp is the pore diameter and C is a geometric constant. Thus, flow through smaller pores faces higher hydraulic resistance, which can attenuate coolant penetration unless pressure is increased.

Boiling behaviour: Pore size also governs the boiling regime by influencing capillary wicking, nucleation site density, and vapor escape dynamics:
•
Smaller pores (e.g., <50 µm): Enhance liquid retention via capillary action but may hinder vapor removal and promote localized dry-out.
•
Larger pores (>100 µm): Facilitate vapor escape and active boiling but may reduce rewetting efficiency during high heat flux conditions.
•
Medium pores (50–100 µm, as used here): Offer a balance between capillary-driven liquid replenishment and sufficient vapor permeability, supporting stable nucleate boiling and mitigating dry-out risks.

Effective thermal resistance: Using the measured temperature rise relative to the inlet (Tcell,max – Tin = 4.8–5.8) and the latent heat removed at 300 mL/min (
m ̇
≈
7.5 g/s,
h
fg
≈
90–11- kJ/kg), the pack-level effective thermal resistance during two-phase operation is Rth,eff
≈ 0.03–0,08 K/W (mid-case ≈ 0.047 K/W for x ≈ 0.15). At matched heat input, the higher-porosity foam (
ε ≈ 0.8
) maps to the lower end of this range, consistent with its greater permeability and vapor venting, which reduce the wall superheat required to sustain nucleate boiling.

The pore structure also helps suppress vapor blanket formation and sustains a high density of nucleation sites, which are crucial for effective two-phase heat transfer. These microfluidic mechanisms underpin the improved thermal uniformity and peak temperature suppression observed in our experiments.

To evaluate the thermal management performance, we used two key metrics. First, the maximum allowable cell temperature, which is critical for battery safety. Second, the temperature difference (ΔT) among cells to ensure uniform heat distribution and aging behaviour. According to the achieved results, our system met both criteria across all cooling configurations, with the porous structure delivering the best results (Tmax = 36.8 °C, ΔTmax = 2.3 °C).

The two-phase immersion cooling system integrated with porous structures exhibited excellent thermal regulation across the entire discharge cycle. As shown in Fig. 10, the temperature evolution of the cells remained smooth and controlled, with no signs of thermal instability or abrupt temperature spikes. This trend indicates that the system maintained a stable two-phase boiling regime. The porous enhancement promotes effective nucleate boiling while retaining coolant near the heat source and enhancing rewetting, thereby significantly reducing the risk of dry-out a known challenge in some two-phase systems under rapid or localized heating. Compared to conventional immersion cooling setups, this design ensures consistent thermal contact and prevents vapor blanketing, contributing to both operational safety and performance reliability.

Fig. 11 illustrates the formation and motion of vapor bubbles within the battery pack as the coolant transitions from liquid to vapour. Bubble nucleation is primarily observed on the top and bottom poles of the battery cells, which reach the saturation temperature of 3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000 (34 °C) earlier than surrounding due to their higher thermal conductivity (metal). The two-phase immersion cooling method provides two key thermal advantages over single-phase cooling: (1) the latent heat of vaporization allows for efficient energy absorption, rapidly reducing surface temperatures, and (2) the continuous boiling process enhances temperature uniformity throughout the pack, minimizing thermal gradients and improving the effectiveness of the overall cooling strategy. The porous media (compression pad) act as preferential heterogeneous nucleation sites, initiating vapor generation at discrete micro-sites. As bubbles form, their buoyancy drives vertical motion through the fluid. In regions where porous compression pads are used, the bubble behaviour is modified by the structure’s microchannels, which guide vapor escape and reduce bubble coalescence. The interconnected pores allow for simultaneous vapor release and capillary-driven liquid replenishment, resulting in a stabilized boiling interface. This continuous exchange suppresses large vapor blankets from forming near the cells, thereby reducing the risk of local overheating or dry-out. These mechanisms directly support the improved thermal uniformity and stability reported across the 24-cell pack and underscore the critical role of both material interfaces and fluid dynamics in two-phase immersion systems.

Pore-scale simulations in the COMSOL Multiphysics 6.3 software, coupling two-phase interface capturing (VOF-type) with laminar flow and teat transfer in fluids was performed to resolve capillary-driven liquid replenishment, vapor venting, and conjugate heat transfer. Phase change was implemented via a latent-heat source term in the energy equation (evaporation/condensation mass source linked to local superheat), with temperature-dependent properties for the dielectric coolant and solids. Porous regions were modelled using a Brinkman–Darcy formulation with an effective thermal conductivity for the solid–fluid matrix; the porosity and average pore sizes were obtained from material characterization. The wettability was imposed through static contact angles (with limited hysteresis sensitivity testing). Boundary conditions reproduced experiments: imposed heat flux at cell walls, liquid mass-flow inlet, pressure outlet, and saturation pressure for two-phase domains. Mesh refinement was concentrated near the solid–fluid interfaces and pores, and a segregated transient solver with adaptive time stepping was used. The model reproduces the measured trends in Tmax and ΔT at 1C, supporting its use for comparative analysis.

It should be noted that the pore-scale simulations prescribe static contact angles at the three-phase line. This choice aligns with the small capillary number, low superheat window of the experiments and with established theory that, at low contact-line speeds, the apparent angle approaches its equilibrium value (Cox–Voinov hydrodynamic law; Blake–De Coninck molecular-kinetic theory) [75]. Using the measured operating conditions for Novec™ 7000 (properties from the 3 M datasheet), we estimate ΔT ≈ 2–4 K near Tsat ≈ 34 °C, μ ≈ 0.45 mPa·s, σ ≈ 0.012–0.0124 N m−1, and porous interstitial velocity U≈(7–9) × 10−3ms−1. The resulting capillary and Jakob numbers are:
(11)
C a =
μ . U
σ
≈
( 2.5 - 3.5 )
×
10
- 4
(12)
J a =
c p
. Δ T
h
fg
≈ 0.02 - 0.04
These small Ca and Ja values place the tests in stable, low-superheat nucleate boiling, where latent heat capping and capillary rewetting, augmented by the porous pads, predominate (consistent with the observed Tmax ≤ 36.8 °C and ΔTmax ≤ 2–3 K). In this regime, published studies indicate that the incremental influence of dynamic contact angle and CAH on onset and early departure is limited for wetting liquids on smooth metals; when present, CAH primarily nudges departure diameter/frequency without altering the regime [76]. A definitive, pore resolved validation of dynamic wettability in random foams would require refractive-index-matched optical access and high-speed imaging/μPIV to resolve contact-line motion and bubble kinematics within the matrix, now an active frontier topic and typically a stand-alone comprehensive study [77]. We have therefore framed the static-angle assumption explicitly, delimited its applicability by dimensionless analysis (Ca, Ja) under our measured conditions, and connected the work to dynamic-angle frameworks that provide a clear pathway for future, dedicated validation.

We quantified bubble-departure dynamics from high-speed clips of the two-phase immersion tests (Novec™ 7000; Tin≈31–32 °C, Tsat≈ 34 °C, 300 mL min−1) at two regions of interest on centrally located cells (top pole and top centre). Using 5 × 5 mm2 region of interests (ROIs) over 10 s windows (≥240 fps), we extracted departure frequency r (events m−2 s−1) and equivalent departure diameter
d
dep
one frame before detachment. The observed sizes were
d

= 100–200 μm. Converting these statistics to a latent-heat flux via [78]:
(13)

yielded values that overlap the electrical heat flux

(55–97 W m−2 at 1C) within 20–30 %. This agreement shows that the measured bubble dynamics are energetically consistent with the heat removal required during discharge. ROI-resolved counts captured the expected spatial heterogeneity of nucleation under porous-pad guidance: event-rate densities were higher at the top poles than at the top centre, consistent with capillary wicking toward the poles and preferential nucleation sites. Despite these local variations, the latent-heat flux integrated over the wetted area matches
q

"
within uncertainty, aligning with the temperature profiles in Fig. 10 and the bubble dynamics (e.g., smaller, frequent departures) illustrated in Fig. 11. These findings further support the modelling assumption of a static contact angle in the pore-scale VOF simulations. Under the present conditions we report Ca≈(2.5–3.5) × 10−4 and Ja≈0.02–0.04, i.e., low-superheat nucleate boiling where dynamic-angle effects weakly perturb departure metrics.

As shown in Fig. 12 , at ε = 0.6, the tighter pore network yields a lower permeability and higher viscous losses, concentrating liquid through a few preferential throats. The velocity field therefore exhibits sharper jets and recirculation pockets, which promotes local localised thermal gradients where convective removal is the weakest. Due to a higher solid fraction, vapor can be more easily trapped at constrictions, intermittently blanketing hot surfaces and increasing interfacial superheat before rewetting occurs. At ε = 0.8, the permeability increases, and the velocity field becomes more spatially uniform, reducing stagnant zones and facilitating both capillary wicking and vapor venting. This comes at the cost of a lower solid fraction (and thus somewhat lower matrix keff, and larger characteristic pores slightly reduce the capillary pressure; nevertheless, for the pore sizes considered here, the net effect is improved liquid supply and vapor removal, decreasing required superheat for sustained boiling.

Introducing the porous layer produces a more uniform two-phase distribution at the heater interface. Fig. 13 shows the vapor volume fractions in the presence and absence of porous structure. Volume fraction fields show a reduced spatial variance of vapor volume fraction and a higher interfacial liquid coverage compared with the non-porous case. Mechanistically, pores act as liquid feeders (capillary wicking) and preferential vapour chimneys, which limits vapor blanket formation, increases the bubble departure frequency (smaller, more frequent events), and enlarges the effective evaporation area. The combined effect is a higher convective/boiling heat-transfer coefficient and delayed dry-out relative to smooth-channel flow boiling under the same nominal conditions.

The results indicate that the porous layer suppresses large-scale coalescence and stabilizes transient oscillations, shortening the rewetting time after local vapor bursts. Quantitatively, this appears as a narrower distribution of interface temperatures and a lower coefficient of variation in vapor fraction in the near-wall control volume. The benefits are counterbalanced by an added pressure drop and, at very high heat flux or very large pores, potential capillary starvation. Within the tested range, the porous medium enhances heat removal primarily by homogenizing the phase distribution and sustaining liquid supply. Tuning the porosity and pore size balances the capillary pressure, permeability, and thermal spreading to maximize the performance.

Fig. 14 compares the performance metrics of the cooling mechanisms tested in this study. The left-hand axis represents the ratio of rejected heat to generated heat (Qout/Qin), while the right-hand axis shows the maximum temperature difference between cells normalized to the inlet temperature (ΔTmax/Tin). It should be noted that while several configurations lead to Tmax below ∼ 60 °C and, for immersion methods, (ΔT of order a few K at 1C), these observations should not be construed as compliance with predefined criteria, they characterize relative performance under the tested conditions only. To ensure fairness in the comparative evaluation, identical electrical boundary conditions were applied across all configurations (1C discharge). The flow rate in coolant circulation for liquid-based methods (cold plate and immersion) was set to 300 mL min−1. For forced convection, we report airflow as the face velocity (m s−1) and the corresponding volumetric flow (CFM) over the test-section area, rather than equating it volumetrically to the liquid flow. Because air and liquids have very different densities and heat capacities, equal volumetric flow rates do not imply equal cooling. The pressure was atmospheric for natural convection and single-phase immersion; two-phase immersion operated at the saturation vapor pressure of the working fluid. Local velocities differ by mode (fan-driven air, pump-driven liquid, vapor generation), but boundary conditions for electrical loading and nominal liquid circulation were kept consistent. Table 6 summarizes the parameters.

As shown, air cooling methods, natural convection (NC) and forced convection (FC), exhibit the lowest heat transfer ratios, indicating their relative ineffectiveness. The top-mounted cold plate has the highest temperature difference and the lowest heat removal ratio among the cold plate configurations. In contrast, the bottom-mounted cold plate achieves the highest heat removal ratio, while cold plates on both the top and bottom surfaces deliver the most uniform temperature distribution across the battery pack, with the lowest temperature differences between cells.

The use of a porous structure for the immersion cooling method reduces cell temperature differences. However, the thermal transport capability (heat removal ratio) for single-phase immersion cooling decreases. This reduction is primarily due to the increased pressure drop and lower flow rates for a given pressure difference across the battery pack. In the two-phase immersion cooling case, however, the porous structure enhances heat removal capability, attributed to reduced flow resistance and the efficiency of the latent heat of vaporization during the phase change process.

As discussed, the two-phase immersion system enhanced with a porous medium delivers superior temperature uniformity and significantly reduced peak cell temperatures. To contextualize its practical efficiency, we calculated the gravimetric thermal performance as 98.7 W/kg and volumetric thermal performance as 73,852 W/m3, based on the estimated mass and volume of the cooling setup. These values reflect the system’s high heat dissipation capability per unit mass and volume a key requirement for applications constrained by space and weight, such as electric aviation and data centre cooling (Table 7 ). The gravimetric and volumetric performance values are computed for the whole system used in testing: the active thermal module (cells, porous medium, in-module heat-exchange hardware, dielectric inventory) together with the integrated balance-of-plant (reservoir, passive condenser/heat-rejection hardware, tubing/fittings/manifolds). Because passive component selection, such as reservoir volume, condenser geometry, tubing length/diameter, and enclosure mass, affects the total mass and volume, these normalizations are sensitive to the chosen architecture and should be interpreted as whole-system figures for the present rig. For further comparative rigor, we also estimated dimensionless parameters, including a Nusselt number exceeding 40, highlighting the effective convective heat transfer, and a Biot number below 0.1, which confirms negligible internal thermal gradients within the battery cells. Together, these dimensional and dimensionless metrics provide a robust basis for fair performance comparisons and benchmark this system well above conventional cold-plate or air-cooled solutions, which typically report volumetric performance below 40,000 W/m3 [78].

Reviews of air-cooled modules report h≈20-80 W/m2 K for module scales L≈30-50 mm and kair ≈0.026 W/m K, implying Nu typically ≲40 (rarely exceeding O (102)) [8,17,20]. Cold-plate (water–glycol) systems collate h≈500-1500 W/m2K, mapped to the same module length, this corresponds to Nu≈25-80 [9,18,21,24]. Immersion approaches raise h: single-phase fluorinated liquids h≈200-800 (Nu≈90-370), and two-phase immersion h≳800-3000 (Nu≳280) [1,2,41,44]. The Nu > 40 (for single phase immersion) and Nu≈170-340 band inferred for this study therefore sits above typical single-phase ranges and within the two-phase regime, consistent with the observed low superheat and near-isothermal behaviour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Engineering Integration, environmental Impact, and practical Considerations
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to demonstrating excellent thermal regulation, the practical deployment of the proposed two-phase immersion cooling system requires a broader evaluation across environmental, electrochemical, and engineering dimensions especially as immersion technologies mature from lab-scale validation to large-scale electric mobility applications.

Although NOVEC™ 7000 and 7100 fluids are widely used in dielectric applications due to their low toxicity, non-flammability, and chemical stability, recent reviews have highlighted their moderate Global Warming Potential (GWP) approximately 530 for NOVEC 7000 and 320 for NOVEC 7100 over a 100-year horizon [83]. While these are substantially lower than legacy PFCs (e.g., CFC-113: GWP ∼ 6000), they are not negligible. Emerging low-GWP candidates include hydro-(fluoro)olefins such as 1233zd(E)/ 1234ze(E) and synthetic ester immersion oils. HFO/HCFOs offer ultra-low GWP, but their boiling points are near or below ambient, and their latent-heat capacities are lower than HFE-class fluids. Two-phase operation around 30–40 °C may therefore require modest back-pressure and careful flammability/compatibility management for certain grades. Synthetic esters (near-zero GWP, readily biodegradable) support single-phase immersion with good specific heat and conductivity, yet they lack latent-heat buffering, typically demanding higher flow and yielding higher Tmax/ΔT at comparable loads. Selection thus balances climate impact, safety classification, and the thermal headroom required by the duty. Still, their zero-ozone depletion potential, short atmospheric lifetimes, and enclosure-contained usage make them viable for mission-critical thermal management, especially when used in closed-loop or sealed systems where fluid loss is minimized [84]. Future implementations may explore HFOs or bio-derived alternatives as potential replacements for further reducing lifecycle emissions [85].

One common concern with immersion cooling is the interaction between coolant fluids and electrochemical cells. Both NOVEC 7000 and 7100 are chemically inert and possess very high dielectric strength (>35 kV), making them ideal candidates for direct-contact cooling of battery modules [33]. During our experiments, no adverse effects were observed on terminal voltage, capacity fade, or coulombic efficiency across multiple discharge cycles. Prior studies confirm that fluorinated dielectric liquids do not penetrate typical cell seals under moderate thermal cycling and that electrochemical degradation is negligible [86]. During the experimental campaign (∼100 charge–discharge cycles, over a six-week period), no visible degradation of the polyurethane porous medium was observed in contact with the fluorinated dielectric fluid. It should be noted that this observation reflects short-term exposure conditions. Long-term immersion studies on data centre applications reported material and fluid stability beyond 1,000 cycles [72,84], indicating generally favourable compatibility of such dielectric fluids with porous materials. Nevertheless, dedicated accelerated aging tests on polyurethane foams in battery immersion contexts remain necessary and are planned as part of future work.

While literature confirms short-term material stability [72,84], battery-specific accelerated-aging data remain scarce. We therefore estimate long-term behaviour using Arrhenius-type accelerated-aging models. For example, Da Silva et al. report a ∼ 15 % decline in polyurethane foam compression modulus after 72 h at 90 °C—commonly equivalent to ∼ 2,000 h at 25 °C [87]. Extrapolating via Arrhenius suggests capillary pressure (linked to pore structure and compression modulus) may decline by ∼ 5–10 % over ∼ 1,000 full-equivalent cycles (≈ one year of repeated thermal cycling), with negligible impact on wicking functionality. Structural age-related effects (e.g., modest shifts in contact angle/porosity) could slightly increase boiling superheat but remain within the safety envelope provided by two-phase latent-heat buffering. In addition, polyurethane aging literature shows minor chemical changes (e.g., increased carbonyl index) under UV/humidity acceleration without significant degradation in surface energy or adhesion [88]. These theoretical predictions are supported by prior battery-specific studies, Choi et al. [89] observed stable polyurethane foam in Novec 7000 immersion over 1,000 + cycles, and Lv et al. [90] reported minimal degradation under prolonged thermal cycling, confirming long-term compatibility of the dielectric fluid–foam system. Finally, permeation of dielectric fluids into cell seals remains a valid concern: although prior studies indicate negligible penetration under moderate thermal cycling, in-situ seal integrity testing after ≥ 1,000 cycles is recommended to confirm long-term compatibility under elevated transient temperatures.

The Arrhenius extrapolation in this study (projected 5–10 % reduction in effective capillary pressure over ∼ 1,000 full-equivalent cycles) is framed as a conservative upper bound. By contrast, the endurance studies cited in [89,90] monitored wicking-relevant proxies, liquid uptake/transport, liquid-holding capacity, and pore-scale morphology, and did not detect statistically significant deterioration under Novec/HFE immersion and repeated thermal cycling in the reported windows. Notably, those works assessed foam integrity primarily via transport and structural endpoints rather than direct capillary-pressure measurement; within their confidence intervals, wicking functionality and pore architecture were retained. Taken together, a modest modulus-related softening predicted by Arrhenius models does not necessarily translate into pore-throat coarsening or loss of imbibition head, which explains the benign transport behaviour observed experimentally. Within the tested duty cycles of this study, the demonstrated two-phase configuration maintained liquid supply to nucleation sites and tight temperature uniformity (ΔT ≤ 3 K), indicating practical headroom even if the conservative Arrhenius bound were approached.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scalability and system integration into High-Density EV modules
ce:section.ce:para: While the current design targets a 24-cell module, the approach remains scalable. The system’s gravimetric (98.7 W/kg) and volumetric (73,852 W/m3) thermal performance metrics suggest significant potential for lightweight, high-power-density platforms, such as electric aviation, UAVs, and performance EVs. For modules exceeding 100 cells, challenges such as uniform fluid distribution, venting for vapor escape, and coolant mass optimization must be considered. Practical scale-up modifications include segmented vapor-vent channels aligned with cell rows, zoned plenums with one-way baffles, and compact vapor–liquid separators at manifold turns to relieve back-pressure and prevent cross-flow choking while preserving nucleate boiling. Prior studies have proposed multi-zone thermal pathways or integrated wicking structures to address these scale-up challenges without compromising weight or reliability [91].

Two-phase systems, while thermally efficient, are inherently sensitive to heat flux spikes and vapor dry-out—a condition where the liquid coolant fully vaporizes at the heat transfer surface and fails to rewet it, potentially leading to thermal runaway [92]. To mitigate this risk, our system integrates porous compression pads between battery cells, which fulfil two critical functions:
1.
Capillary-driven liquid retention, ensuring continuous coolant supply to local localised thermal gradients and delaying dry-out, and
2.
Boiling stabilization, by increasing nucleation site density and supporting uniform bubble generation and detachment.

These enhancements collectively sustain a stable nucleate boiling regime, even under elevated thermal loads. The temperature evolution curves presented in Fig. 10 confirm this behaviour, showing smooth, repeatable thermal profiles throughout the discharge cycle. The maximum cell temperature remains below 36.8 °C, well within the safe margin above the boiling point of 3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000 (34 °C). Additionally, the temperature gradient across the pack remains below 2.3 °C, indicating spatial thermal uniformity and effective vapor–liquid coexistence.

From a theoretical perspective, Critical Heat Flux (CHF) marks the upper limit of safe boiling operation. While Zuber’s classical model offers CHF predictions for saturated pool boiling on flat surfaces, the geometry and flow conditions in immersion-cooled battery packs are more accurately described by flow boiling CHF correlations, such as those proposed by Kandlikar and Lienhard–Dhir, which account for effects of mass flux, subcooling, confinement, and fluid-surface interactions.

Extensive studies [93] have shown that porous media can enhance CHF by a factor of 2–5, owing to:
•
Enhanced capillary-driven rewetting that prevents localized vapor blanketing,
•
Suppressed bubble coalescence, promoting distributed nucleation and delaying transition to film boiling, and
•
Improved vapor escape paths, reducing thermal resistance at the liquid–vapor interface.

These effects are especially pronounced in open-cell polyurethane foams like those used in this study, with pore sizes of 50–100 μm and porosities of 60–80 %, which maximize coolant accessibility and interfacial area for phase change. While no signs of dry-out or boiling instability were observed during our test cycles, future experiments under transient loading conditions, higher C-rates, or partial coolant loss scenarios will be pursued to further validate the system’s resilience and safety margins.

While two-phase immersion cooling offers distinct thermal advantages—including superior heat flux management and temperature uniformity—its deployment introduces several important engineering trade-offs in terms of system integration, complexity, and long-term maintenance.

A primary engineering consideration lies in the enclosure design. Immersion cooling systems require sealed, chemically compatible housings capable of containing dielectric fluids such as 3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000 or 7100 over extended lifetimes. These housings must withstand thermal cycling, vibration, and internal pressure variations, especially in mobile environments like electric vehicles (EVs). The enclosure materials must be resistant to swelling, permeation, or chemical breakdown, and sealing techniques must prevent microleakage or evaporation, particularly under elevated ambient temperatures.

Coolant selection is another critical design factor that directly influences operational performance and safety. Suitable immersion fluids must exhibit low viscosity, high dielectric strength, low global warming potential (GWP), and stable thermophysical properties under prolonged boiling and cycling. In this study, 3 M™ NOVEC™ 7000 was selected due to its favourable balance of thermal conductivity (0.069 W/m·K), low boiling point (34 °C), chemical inertness, and established use in electronics cooling. However, fluid properties must be matched carefully with the system's expected heat fluxes, pressure envelope, and material compatibility. For instance, fluids with lower boiling points enable early phase change and uniform temperature capping but may pose higher evaporation risks, necessitating enhanced sealing and vapor recovery. In future implementations, dynamic regulation of fluid temperature and saturation pressure—via passive condenser tuning or fluid blends, could provide further control over system thermal response and fluid longevity. Furthermore, performance must be evaluated over the fluid's lifecycle, including monitoring for degradation, particulate buildup, or additive loss, which could compromise boiling efficiency or insulation.

Another challenge is vapour management. In two-phase systems, local boiling generates vapor bubbles that must be effectively separated and recondensed to maintain thermal stability and avoid pressure buildup. Our passive system design avoids active circulation or vapor compressors by relying on capillary structures and gravity-assisted separation. However, this requires careful internal design of the vapor space, venting routes, and passive condenser locations, which become critical to system safety and performance under varying orientations and load conditions.

The serviceability of immersion-cooled battery packs is more limited than air- or cold-plate-cooled configurations. Because cells are submerged in fluid, routine inspection, diagnostics, and cell replacement become more complicated. To address this, future designs may adopt modular immersion trays, fluid drainage ports, and quick-connect sealing mechanisms that facilitate maintenance without full disassembly. In addition, dielectric fluid monitoring will be essential to ensure long-term performance, using inline sensors or sampling-based refractometry and chemical analysis to detect degradation, contamination, or loss of dielectric properties.

Despite these challenges, immersion cooling also delivers key engineering benefits. The absence of mechanical pumps, fans, or moving thermal interfaces translates into a lower part count and fewer failure modes, improving overall system reliability—particularly important in vibration-prone, safety–critical platforms. The thermal contact resistance is also minimized, as coolant directly interfaces with the cell surfaces, improving heat transfer and simplifying pack architecture.

Importantly, the economic case for immersion cooling has already been demonstrated in the data centre industry, where studies show that although the initial capital expenditure is higher, long-term benefits include lower energy consumption, higher component longevity, and reduced maintenance frequency [94]. These advantages are beginning to translate into electric mobility and aerospace sectors, particularly in fast-charging EVs, electric aviation, and high-performance battery systems, where thermal limits, space constraints, and weight sensitivity dominate design priorities.

Recent two-phase and forced-immersion studies report strong thermal control examples include SF-33 and Novec-based two-phase tests that maintain cell temperatures near saturation (≈33–36 °C) and ΔT values of a few °C under moderate to high C-rates [79]. Typical literature volumetric and gravimetric figures cluster around 60,000–70,000 W/m3 and 70–90 W/kg depending on cell format, coolant, and pack density [41,47,82]. Our reported metrics (73,852 W/m3 and 98.7 W/kg) therefore exceed these representative benchmarks, which we attribute to the combined benefits of two-phase latent-heat extraction and porous-media enhancement (which improves nucleation, liquid retention and vapor venting). We also note the engineering trade-offs: porous integration brings modest added mechanical complexity and some pressure-drop penalties, but the approach reduces reliance on heavy manifolds and high-power pumping and materially improves two-phase stability under the tested conditions.

To provide practical context for system selection we performed a conservative, pack-level lifecycle cost comparison that includes capital and operating components relevant to coolant replenishment and porous-media replacement for immersion, and hardware + pumping for cold plates. We base calculations on the 24-cell pack used in this study (nominal energy ≈ 0.311 kWh per pack), published fluid prices for 3 M™ Novec™ fluids, and representative cold-plate hardware prices. Industry guidance for leak testing and production-level leak tightness is cited (IEC 62660–2 for test methodology [95]; INFICON/industry guidance [96] for typical leak-rate thresholds). Full assumptions and calculation steps are provided below (Table 8 ).

Although unit prices were drawn from technical and academic sources, the bulk-procurement cost methodology adopted in this study is as follows. List prices for 3 M™ Novec™ fluids and polyurethane foams were taken from peer-reviewed technical sources [97–99] and treated as baseline values plist. To reflect economies of scale typical of industrial procurement, a bulk-discount factor

. These discounts are commercially negotiated and vary by supplier, geography, and volume. Three procurement tiers were assumed and analysed: Lab/Pilot (0–5 % discount; ≤5–10 L), Pre-production (10–20 %; ∼50–200 L), and Production (25–35 %; ≥200 L). The initial fluid cost per pack was computed as
C fluid

, using the measured fill volume

and Novec™ density ρ . The same discount bands were applied to foam sheets, although their contribution remained small at pack level. Lifecycle cost figures are reported as ranges across these tiers to maintain transparency regarding price uncertainty and purchasing scale.

Under conservative assumptions (10-year amortization, 1 % annual fluid replenishment, foam replacement every 3–5 years, pump energy use), the estimated lifecycle cooling cost for porous-enhanced two-phase immersion is ≈ $0.12–0.25 / kWh, while a representative cold-plate solution is ≈ $0.08–0.20 / kWh. The ranges reflect uncertainty in local fluid pricing, pack duty cycle, and maintenance schedules; importantly, the dominant cost items differ (initial dielectric inventory and enclosure controls for immersion; pumping energy and manifold/cold-plate capital for cold plates). We therefore conclude that the porous-enhanced two-phase approach is competitive on a lifecycle $/kWh basis while offering superior thermal performance; however, deployment requires robust leakage testing and enclosure design in accordance with IEC/industry guidance.

This study comprehensively evaluated the performances of various cooling mechanisms for lithium-ion battery cells, including natural and forced convection and cooling plates, as well as single-phase immersion cooling and two-phase immersion cooling, with and without porous media. The results demonstrate the advantages and limitations of each method, providing critical insights into their suitability for advanced battery thermal management systems. The main conclusions drawn from this study can be listed as:
•
While simple and cost-effective, natural convection proved to be inadequate for effective thermal regulation. The battery cells reached temperatures of approximately 60 °C, well above the optimal operating range, with uneven heat dissipation further exacerbating thermal gradients. Forced convection offered a modest improvement, reducing peak temperatures to around 40 °C. However, similar issues of non-uniform cooling and insufficient mitigation of hot spots persisted, making this method unsuitable for high-performance applications.
•
Cooling plates offered a more efficient solution, reducing cell temperatures to approximately 35 °C in regions of direct contact. However, their inability to manage heat effectively in the middle of the battery pack, where physical contact with the plates was absent, limited their performance. These findings highlight the importance of uniform heat distribution across the entire battery structure.
•
Single-phase immersion cooling exhibited better temperature regulation and uniformity, particularly when enhanced with a porous structure. However, the low thermal conductivity of the compression pad used in the porous media led to elevated surface temperatures. This limitation underscores the need for high-conductivity materials to realize the full potential of this cooling approach.
•
The two-phase immersion cooling system led to the most effective thermal management. By leveraging the latent heat of vaporization, the coolant efficiently absorbed excess heat whenever the surface temperature approached 36 °C, preventing further increases and maintaining a stable thermal profile. This capability ensured optimal operating temperatures and significantly enhanced temperature uniformity across the battery pack, addressing key challenges other methods face.
•
This study also introduces and evaluates gravimetric (98.7 W/kg) and volumetric (73,852 W/m3) thermal performance metrics, emphasizing the compactness and efficiency of the two-phase immersion cooling system with porous media. Achieving such performance without complex circulation or refrigeration systems highlights its practicality for electric mobility applications, where space, weight, and reliability are paramount.
•
This study establishes a comparative baseline at 1C. Future studies will provide an extension to 3C–4C with upgraded current delivery and coolant control to validate the scalability and assess transient cooling robustness under ultra-fast charging conditions. The PASS will be declare a PASS only when all of the following are met: (i) peak cell temperature Tmax < 38 °C at 3C and Tmax < 40 °C at 4C; (ii) pack non-uniformity ΔT ≤ 3 K over the 10–90 % SOC window; (iii) flow stability with RMS flow ripple ≤ 5 % and outlet void-fraction ripple ≤ 0.05; and (iv) critical-heat-flux margin ≥ 2X the applied duty heat flux. Failure of any single gate constitutes FAIL and triggers redesign of manifolding/porous media or operating set-points. These gates align with best practice in two-phase BTMS studies and IEC leak-testing guidance.

While this study employed a 1C discharge rate, chosen to ensure precise thermal characterization and cross-method comparability, we recognize that 1C does not fully represent fast-charging EV scenarios (2C–4C). The 1C baseline remains a widely used standard for comparative thermal benchmarking and is particularly relevant where the thermal safety and cycle life are prioritized (e.g., stationary storage, light-duty EVs, UAVs). Within the next 12 months we will extend the 24-cell protocol to 3C–4C and validate performance on the same rigs used for the 1C benchmark. In fast-rate extensions, feasibility is framed by explicit safety envelopes: a conservative thermal cap of

Based on 1C data and prior two-phase literature, we anticipate: air cooling > 50–60 °C with large ΔT [100]; cold plates capping Tmax at ∼ 38–45 °C at 3C with centre-hot risks unless manifolding/ flow are optimised [101]; single-phase immersion (Novec 7100/HFE-7100) to low-40 s °C depending on Tin and flow [33]; and two-phase immersion (Novec 7000) with porous pads holding Tmax near Tsat (∼34–36 °C) with pack-level ΔT ≤ 3 K up to 4C ([102]). PASS will be declared only if all gates are met: i) peak cell temperature satisfies Tmax ≤ 40 °C at 3C and, for two-phase immersion, ii) Tmax ≤ 38 °C at 4C; iii) pack non-uniformity remains ΔT ≤ 3 K; and iv) the critical-heat-flux margin is at least 2 × the applied duty heat flux. Tests will span Tin = 30–34 °C and, for immersion modes, coolant flow of 0.3–0.5 L min−1 with ± 15° tilt and step-load perturbations. Failure to satisfy any single gate constitutes FAIL and triggers redesign of manifolding/porous media or operating set-points. These parameter ranges were selected based on prior two-phase immersion cooling studies using Novec-series dielectric fluids ([41,47,81,82,102], which identified 30–35 °C inlet conditions and sub-1 L min−1 flow regimes as optimal for sustaining stable nucleate boiling and reflecting practical fast-charging module operation. These settings also maintain

and
Nu ≈ 170 - 340
in the two-phase configuration at the reported Reynolds numbers and inlet subcooling, consistent with immersion-cooling benchmarks that balance low thermal resistance with uniformity during fast-rate operation. In conclusion, the two-phase immersion cooling system emerged as the superior battery thermal management solution. Its ability to regulate temperatures efficiently and uniformly while mitigating hot spots makes it an ideal candidate for next-generation battery systems. The findings provide valuable guidance for designing and implementing advanced cooling strategies, paving the way for safer, more reliable, and higher-performance battery packs in electric vehicles and other applications.
